title: Following the Dirty Money
subtitle: How money laundering actually works

hook.metaphor: Dirty money has a problem: it can't explain itself. A suitcase of cash from crime can't just be deposited and spent — the questions would sink the criminal. So laundering is like running dirty laundry through a wash: get it *into* the machine, spin it around until nobody can trace the stains, then fold it neatly back into normal life. Three steps, every time.
hook.scenario: A criminal has ₹5 crore in cash from illegal sales. He can't bank it in one go without setting off alarms. Over months it becomes: dozens of small cash deposits, a maze of transfers through shell companies abroad, and finally a 'clean' apartment and a 'profitable' restaurant. Trace the three stages.

learn: Money laundering classically has **three stages.** Learn these three words and you understand the whole game: **placement, layering, integration.**

**Placement** is getting the dirty cash *into* the financial system — the riskiest step, because raw cash is conspicuous. Criminals break it into small deposits (**structuring** / 'smurfing') to stay under reporting thresholds, or push it through cash-heavy businesses.

**Layering** is the spin cycle: moving the money through many transactions — transfers, shell companies, foreign accounts, crypto, buying and selling assets — to break the trail between the money and its criminal source. The more confusing the maze, the harder to follow.

**Integration** is the money returning to the criminal looking clean and legitimate — as 'business profits', property, or investment returns they can spend openly. Our criminal's apartment and restaurant are integration. The defender's job is to spot the wash at any stage — and crucially, to file a report when something looks off. (Note: criminals also do the reverse — **terrorist financing** — moving *clean* money toward harm; AML and counter-terrorist-financing, 'CFT', are policed together.)
bridge: So at work, you can recognise the shape of laundering instead of just sensing 'something's dodgy.' Ask: *Is dirty money getting in (placement)? Being shuffled to hide its trail (layering)? Or coming back as fake-legit wealth (integration)?* Naming the stage tells you what red flags to expect — and structuring, shells, and unexplained wealth are the classic tells.

interactions.title: Name the stage
interactions.prompt: Sort each action into the laundering stage it belongs to.

items.label: Making many small cash deposits under the alert threshold
Wiring funds through shell companies in several countries
Buying an apartment with the now-'clean' funds
Depositing crime cash through a cash-heavy car wash
Rapid buy/sell of assets to muddy the trail
Drawing a 'salary' from a fake-profitable restaurant

interactions.title: Spot the smurf
interactions.prompt: A new customer makes nine cash deposits of ₹49,000 across three branches in two days (just under a ₹50,000 reporting threshold). The right read?

options.label: Normal — each deposit is under the limit, so it's fine.
options.feedback: Staying *just* under the threshold, repeatedly, across branches, is the textbook tell of structuring (smurfing). The pattern is the red flag, not any single deposit.

options.label: Likely structuring at the placement stage — investigate and consider a report.
options.feedback: Exactly. Deliberately breaking cash into sub-threshold chunks to dodge reporting is classic placement-stage laundering. This is what monitoring is built to catch.

options.label: It's integration, the final stage.
options.feedback: Right instinct that it's suspicious, wrong stage. Getting cash *in* via sub-threshold deposits is placement; integration is when it returns looking legitimate.

interactions.title: Laundering vocabulary
interactions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.right: Getting dirty cash into the financial system
Shuffling money through transactions to hide its source
Returning the money as apparently legitimate wealth
Splitting cash into sub-threshold deposits to dodge reporting
A company with no real business, used to obscure flows

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Money laundering is a three-step wash — get the dirty money in (placement), shuffle it to hide the trail (layering), then bring it back looking clean (integration).”

checkpoint.q: The three classic stages of money laundering are…
checkpoint.explain: Get it in (placement), hide the trail (layering), bring it back clean (integration).

checkpoint.q: Making many deposits just under a reporting threshold is called…
checkpoint.explain: Structuring deliberately breaks cash into sub-threshold chunks to avoid triggering reports — a placement-stage red flag.

checkpoint.q: Terrorist financing differs from laundering in that it often moves…
options: Clean money toward causing harm (rather than hiding dirty money)
checkpoint.explain: Laundering disguises dirty money's origin; terrorist financing can move clean funds toward harm — hence AML/CFT together.
